html:
TPO Membrane Systems for the Distribution and Supplier-Park Roofs Around Spartanburg

TPO has become the default single-ply membrane on new low-slope construction across the I-85 corridor, and for good reason on the buildings we see here. The warehouses feeding BMW's supplier network in Greer and Duncan, the cross-dock facilities near Wellford, and the retail boxes along Westgate all favor a membrane that installs fast, welds reliably, and holds up under a humid subtropical summer without turning into a maintenance problem.

Membrane Thickness and Reflectivity

We quote TPO in 45, 60, and 80 mil depending on exposure, foot traffic, and the manufacturer's warranty tier the owner wants to carry. Thicker membrane costs more up front but resists puncture and abrasion better on roofs with frequent rooftop unit servicing, which describes most manufacturing and distribution buildings in this market.

Reflectivity matters here too. A white or light-colored TPO membrane keeps rooftop temperatures down through July and August, which reduces thermal cycling on the membrane itself and can ease load on rooftop HVAC equipment serving climate-controlled warehouse or production space.

Attachment Method and Wind Uplift

We select mechanically attached, fully adhered, or induction-welded attachment based on the building's height, exposure category, and the wind uplift pressures calculated under ASCE 7. Open, flat terrain around industrial parks near I-85 can generate higher edge and corner uplift pressures than a sheltered downtown site, so we do not default to one attachment method across every job.

Fastening patterns, plate spacing, and perimeter and corner enhancement are documented in the submittal package we prepare for GCs and building owners, matched to the specific wind zone and building geometry rather than a generic pattern.

Seam Welding and Field Quality Control

TPO seams are hot-air welded, and weld quality is the single biggest variable in how a TPO roof performs over its service life. We probe test seams as installation proceeds rather than waiting until the job is finished, and we document weld width and probe results for the closeout package. Humid Upstate summers add moisture to the equation during installation, which is one reason weld verification during the work matters more than a visual check afterward.

  • Deck inspection and moisture scan before membrane goes down
  • Insulation layout for code-required R-value and drainage taper where specified
  • Membrane attachment matched to calculated wind uplift zones
  • Hot-air welded seams with in-progress probe testing
  • Flashing detail at penetrations, curbs, and perimeter edge metal
  • Final walk-through and manufacturer warranty inspection where required

Manufacturer Warranty Coverage

Most owners in this market want an NDL, or no dollar limit, warranty from the membrane manufacturer, which typically requires installation by an approved contractor and a post-installation inspection. We handle that inspection process as part of the closeout rather than leaving it to the owner to schedule separately, and we provide the warranty term sheet with the bid so procurement teams know what they are buying before the contract is signed.

Warranty length and coverage terms vary by membrane thickness and attachment method, which is one more reason we spec those details up front instead of leaving them to be decided during construction. Some manufacturers also require periodic inspections to keep a long-term warranty active, and we make sure owners understand that requirement before the contract is signed rather than after a claim gets denied for a missed inspection years down the road.

Recover Versus Tear-Off

On existing buildings, TPO can sometimes go down as a recover over an existing single-ply or gravel-surfaced built-up roof, provided the deck and insulation underneath are dry and sound. We evaluate that with core cuts and moisture scanning before recommending recover over tear-off, since installing new membrane over a wet substrate just moves the problem underneath the new roof instead of solving it.

Coordinating Around Production Schedules

A distribution or manufacturing roof tied into the automotive supply chain rarely has the luxury of a full facility shutdown for reroofing. We sequence phased tear-off and dry-in in sections, coordinating with the facility's operations team so loading dock access, rooftop equipment servicing, and production lines below keep running while the roof work proceeds overhead. That sequencing takes more planning up front than a single-pass tear-off, but it is what keeps a supplier-park building operating on schedule.

We also account for staging and material delivery logistics specific to large industrial sites, since a warehouse roof measured in acres requires a different crane and material-hoisting plan than a standard retail box.

Questions We Get From Facilities Teams

What TPO thickness do you recommend for a distribution warehouse with regular rooftop traffic?

60 mil is our typical recommendation where technicians service rooftop units regularly. 45 mil works on roofs with minimal foot traffic and a tighter budget.

How do you determine wind uplift requirements for an industrial park building?

We calculate uplift pressure from building height, exposure category, and roof zone under ASCE 7, then match attachment method and fastening pattern to those numbers in the bid.

Can TPO go over an existing built-up roof?

Sometimes, if core cuts show a dry substrate and the deck is structurally sound. We confirm this before quoting a recover rather than assuming it will work.

Do you provide the manufacturer's NDL warranty inspection?

Yes, we schedule and complete that inspection as part of project closeout and provide the resulting warranty documentation to the owner.

How does Upstate SC humidity affect TPO seam welding?

Moisture in the air and on the membrane surface can affect weld quality, so we monitor conditions during welding and probe test seams as work proceeds rather than only at the end.
